skill.name: html-injection-testing
skill.description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "test for HTML injection", "inject HTML into web pages", "perform HTML injection attacks", "deface web applications", or "test conten...
skill.summary: Comprehensive guide for identifying and exploiting HTML injection vulnerabilities in web applications through authorized security testing.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate educational security testing skill for authorized HTML injection vulnerability assessment. Static findings are false positives or expected content for security education. The skill teaches penetration testing methodologies for identifying and reporting HTML injection flaws in web applications. All examples are clearly educational and the skill includes proper remediation guidance.

medium_findings.title: innerHTML Assignment Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill contains examples of vulnerable innerHTML usage at lines 429 and 433. These are part of the 'Prevention and Remediation' section showing what NOT to do - they are educational examples of vulnerable patterns, not actual vulnerabilities in the skill itself.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution in Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill contains curl command examples for testing. These are standard security testing tools used for authorized vulnerability assessment. All examples target 'target.com' which is a placeholder domain, not real systems.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Examples
low_findings.description: Examples contain URLs to 'attacker.com' and similar domains. These are standard educational placeholders used in security training. No actual malicious infrastructure is referenced.

low_findings.title: Encoding and Obfuscation Techniques
low_findings.description: The skill documents bypass techniques using various encoding methods (URL, HTML entities, Unicode). This is standard educational content for understanding filter evasion in security testing.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Windows SAM Database Detection
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged 'Windows SAM database' at lines 244 and 259. Upon review, these are false positives - the text contains 'SECURITY TESTER' and 'SECURITY VULNERABILITY DETECTED' which triggered the heuristic. No actual SAM database references exist.

dangerous_patterns.title: Educational Code Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains example payloads for HTML injection testing. These are essential educational content for security professionals learning vulnerability assessment. All examples are clearly labeled as educational and target placeholder domains.

content.value_statement: Web applications often fail to properly sanitize user input, allowing attackers to inject malicious HTML content. This skill provides comprehensive methodology for identifying HTML injection flaws through authorized security testing.
actual_capabilities: Identify HTML injection points in web applications through manual and automated testing
Test for stored, reflected, and URL-based HTML injection vulnerabilities
Construct phishing and defacement payloads for security assessment
Bypass basic input filters using encoding and obfuscation techniques
Use Burp Suite and OWASP ZAP for automated vulnerability scanning
Provide remediation recommendations including output encoding and input validation
limitations: Does not exploit identified vulnerabilities beyond demonstration
Does not bypass advanced WAF configurations
Relies on manual verification for complex injection scenarios

use_cases.title: Security Consultant Assessing Client Web Applications
use_cases.description: A penetration tester conducting authorized security assessment for a client needs to identify HTML injection vulnerabilities in their web applications and provide a comprehensive report with remediation steps.

use_cases.title: Developer Learning Web Security
use_cases.description: A web developer wants to understand HTML injection vulnerabilities to write more secure code and properly validate user input in their applications.

use_cases.title: QA Engineer Testing Input Handling
use_cases.description: A QA engineer needs to verify that the application's input handling properly sanitizes HTML content and prevents injection attacks.

prompt_templates.title: Basic HTML Injection Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the html-injection-testing skill to help me test if our application's search function is vulnerable to HTML injection. The search parameter is 'q' and the URL is http://example.com/search
prompt_templates.scenario: Testing a simple search parameter for HTML injection

prompt_templates.title: Stored Injection Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me test for stored HTML injection in the user profile bio field using the html-injection-testing skill. What test payloads should I use and how do I verify if the injection is stored?
prompt_templates.scenario: Testing stored XSS/HTML injection in user-generated content

prompt_templates.title: Bypass Filter Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Our application claims to filter HTML tags. Using the html-injection-testing skill, what encoding and bypass techniques should I test to verify if the filter can be evaded?
prompt_templates.scenario: Testing filter evasion for HTML injection

prompt_templates.title: Remediation Verification
prompt_templates.prompt: After implementing input sanitization, how can I use the html-injection-testing skill to verify that the remediation is effective and no HTML injection vectors remain?
prompt_templates.scenario: Verifying security fixes are properly implemented

output_examples.input: Test search parameter for HTML injection
output: Phase 1: Identified injection point at /search?q= parameter

Phase 2: Basic payload test results:

Test

renders as heading - VULNERABLE
Phase 3: Stored injection confirmed - payload persists after page reload
Recommendation: Implement output encoding using htmlspecialchars() or DOMPurify

output_examples.input: What phishing payloads can demonstrate HTML injection risk?
output: Demonstration payload: Overlay form capturing credentials
Impact: Attacker can harvest user credentials via fake login form
Severity: Medium to High depending on authentication value
Remediation: Strict input validation and CSP headers

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization before testing any web application
Document all findings with proof-of-concept screenshots and request/response pairs
Test both GET and POST parameters, including hidden fields and cookies
Include remediation guidance in your report to help developers fix issues
anti_patterns: Testing production systems without explicit authorization
Using discovered vulnerabilities to access or exfiltrate data
Focusing only on automated tools without manual verification
Reporting findings without providing clear reproduction steps

faq.question: What is the difference between HTML injection and XSS?
faq.answer: HTML injection allows attackers to inject malicious HTML content into web pages, affecting only the page appearance. XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) allows execution of JavaScript code, which is more severe as it can steal cookies, session tokens, or perform actions on behalf of the user.

faq.question: Can HTML injection lead to account compromise?
faq.answer: Yes, through phishing attacks. Attackers can inject fake login forms that appear legitimate and capture user credentials when submitted.

faq.question: What tools are recommended for HTML injection testing?
faq.answer: Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP, and manual testing with curl are the primary tools. Automated scanners can find basic injection points but manual testing is needed for complex scenarios.

faq.question: How do I prevent HTML injection in my web application?
faq.answer: Use output encoding (htmlspecialchars in PHP, escape in Python), implement Content Security Policy headers, validate input against allowlists, and use modern frameworks that auto-escape by default.

faq.question: Is testing on localhost considered ethical?
faq.answer: Testing on your own systems or systems you own is generally acceptable. Always ensure you have explicit written authorization for any system you do not own.

faq.question: What is the typical severity of HTML injection?
faq.answer: HTML injection is typically medium severity since it cannot execute JavaScript directly. However, when combined with phishing or defacement, the impact can be high, especially on sites with user authentication.
